H1-009-C1.key_attack: Concentration estimate error in generation: 215 uM figure inflated 7x from cold-shock Gonyaulax data; corrected range 1-30 uM. Cold-shock vs heat-shock context unverified for Symbiodiniaceae.

H2-009-C1.key_attack: No molecular target for melatonin-PSII electron transport link. Roopin 2013 was ambient temperature (25C), not thermal stress. Cytotoxicity vs protection ambiguity unresolved -- Fv/Fm under melatonin at 32C not measured.

H3-009-C1.key_attack: Internal contradiction: D-clade (tolerant) inhabits low-light environments where photocycle-driven nocturnal peaks are weakest. TPH thermal inhibition at 32-34C unsupported -- likely inverted (Q10 kinetics). Antolín 1997 shows UPREGULATION under stress, contradicting depletion model.

key_counter_evidence.concentration_calculation_error: Antolín 1997 reports 50 ng/mg protein under cold-shock. Corrected conversion using 150 mg protein/mL cell volume gives ~32 uM, not 215 uM. Generation calculation assumed 1 mg protein/mL (7x error).
key_counter_evidence.cold_shock_vs_heat_shock: Antolín 1997 model is temperature DROP (20C to 15C). Whether Symbiodiniaceae UPREGULATE melatonin under temperature RISE (heat stress) is entirely untested -- may behave oppositely.
key_counter_evidence.D_clade_low_light_habitat: Durusdinium trenchii (thermally tolerant clade D) disproportionately abundant in turbid, low-light environments (LaJeunesse et al. Symbiodiniaceae ecology). Photocycle-dependent nocturnal peaks require high light/dark contrast -- weakest in D-clade habitat.
key_counter_evidence.TPH_thermal_kinetics: Mammalian TPH2 optimal at 37C. Ectotherm TPH follows Q10 kinetics upward to denaturation. No evidence for TPH inhibition at 32-34C in coral-associated dinoflagellates.
key_counter_evidence.depletion_vs_upregulation: Antolín 1997 demonstrates INCREASED melatonin under stress in Gonyaulax, contradicting the progressive depletion model in H3.
key_counter_evidence.roopin_ambient_temperature: Roopin 2013 experiments at ~25C ambient, not thermal stress. Photosynthesis reduction may reflect circadian melatonin physiology (nocturnal suppression of daytime photosynthesis) rather than thermal stress protection.

critic_questions: H1 concentration correction: recalculate with 1-30 uM corrected range instead of 215 uM -- does the OH scavenging fraction still provide meaningful protection at 10 uM via cascade?

H2 molecular target: propose any candidate molecular target through which melatonin could reduce PSII electron throughput in Symbiodiniaceae -- redox kinase, sigma-like receptor, cytochrome b6f allosteric regulator?

H3 reframing: abandon D-clade prediction (internally contradicted by low-light habitat). Reframe as: high-light Symbiodiniaceae populations have higher nocturnal melatonin amplitude regardless of clade identity
Shared measurement priority: HPLC-MS melatonin in D1a vs B1 at 26C vs 32C (24h) is the single experiment that would most efficiently test all three hypotheses simultaneously

Host melatonin contribution: Roopin and Levy 2012 (PMID 22506978, 23300630) documented melatonin in basal metazoans including cnidarians -- does the coral HOST independently produce melatonin that protects symbionts?

meta_critique.common_weakness: All three hypotheses depend on unmeasured Symbiodiniaceae melatonin concentrations under thermal stress -- single HPLC-MS measurement would partially resolve all three
meta_critique.strongest_hypothesis: H1-009-C1 -- most actionable (PRJNA723630 exists), two strong anchor papers, quantitatively defensible for hydroxyl radical scavenging
meta_critique.reframe_opportunity: H3 can be rescued by removing the D-clade-specific prediction and reframing as light-regime-dependent nocturnal amplitude
meta_critique.under_attacked: H2 controlled shutdown is genuinely novel -- photoprotective mechanisms are often characterized behaviorally before molecular targets identified (PsbS precedent)
